dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for chest pain and possible stroke at a residence in Richmond due to a reported headache and altered mental status.
Hi, I'm Brian, an EMT with the rescue squad. Is there any immediate danger to the patient?
The patient is experiencing a headache, dizziness, and altered mental status. Symptoms started about 30 minutes ago.
Ma'am, can you tell me your name and where you are?
My name is Sarah, and I'm at home in the bedroom.
What day is today, and where are we?
She is squeezing both of my hands forcefully, though her speech is a little confused.
I can visualize the airway, and it is patent. No signs of obstruction.
Respirations are unlabored and at a rate of 16 per minute.
Chest expansion appears equal with no signs of tracheal deviation.
Radial pulse is 72, regular, and strong. Peripheral pulses are present bilaterally.
Blood pressure reading is 193 over 127. That's stage 2 hypertension.
Pulse remains 72, and is consistent with regular rhythm.
Can you tell me if your headache is localized or diffuse? Any other associated symptoms?
It's all over my head, no other symptoms that I can recall.
You mentioned no allergies. Is there any history of allergic reactions?
No known drug allergies on record from her discharge papers.
She has diabetes and hypertension. Is she on insulin? Does she take other meds regularly?
Not that I can tell, but I'm not certain of the exact medications.
What about a history of stroke, seizures, or other conditions?
She had a prior stroke, also diabetes, and a diagnosis of seizures.
When was the last meal or drink you had before you felt unwell?
She had water two hours ago. I'm not sure about her food intake today.
Did anything specific happen just before her symptoms began?
Symptoms started about 30 minutes ago without a specific activity noted.
Can we have her age or any other identifiers from the paperwork?
She's 83 years old and her birthday is in April.
On a 0-10 scale, with 10 being the worst possible, how would you rank the pain in her head?
Maybe an 8. It's persistent and not getting any better.
Glucose is reading 145 mg/dL initially. The second reading shows no result, could be device error or hypoglycemia but no further action performed at this time.
Let me shine a light in her eyes for a pupillary exam. They appear non-reactive and constricted at 1 mm.
No signs of JVD or tracheal deviation. No slurred speech noted, though confusion is present per family about place and time.
Lung sounds are clear bilaterally. No abnormal work of breathing noted at this time.
No audible abnormal lung sounds or chest asymmetry observed with respiration efforts.
Her oxygen saturation is at 99% on room air and remains stable.
Attaching four-lead ECG now. The machine will monitor her cardiac rhythm.
We need to use full PPE per protocol for this call. Is it okay to enter the bedroom?
Proceed, we are ready.
Skin is warm, dry, intact. No signs of rashes or depressions in skin turgor.
Noting fluid retention in both ankles. She ambulates with assist and shows lower limb weakness.
No deformities or significant bruising seen in either upper or lower limbs. No signs of trauma found in the survey team's review of the body.
Second reading is no result, device error, and no hypoglycemic signs observed on patient. No further test at this time.
Oxygen saturation has decreased to 95%, but the patient remains stable and does not require oxygen supplementation at this time.
Latest measured blood pressure is 187 over 116. Still abnormal but no emergency signs observed in the field for now.
We are going to perform 12-lead ECG now as per protocol for stroke protocol considerations.
12-lead results show sinus rhythm with frequent PVCs and left axis deviation. Left ventricular hypertrophy is noted on the printout.
Per protocol, initiating a Stroke/Code Neuro Alert for possible infarction and CVA en route to medical facility.
IV access not obtained due to poor vascular access.
DCAP-BTLS survey negative. No wounds, lacerations, or signs of trauma reported in field observations.
Left ventricular hypertrophy is recorded on the ECG, reinforcing the need for neurology decision-making in transit.
Based on neurological concern and ECG findings, this call is consistent with stroke protocol and requires prompt transport.
Transporting to VCU for neurological concerns, confirmed for possible CVA suspicion.
She's being moved via stair chair first, then to the stretcher once secured.
Patient is now stabilized, secured and ready for ambulance transfer.
Call in Stroke Alert to ED for this 83-year-old female with a code neuro suspicion and acute headache, dizziness with altered mental status.
Patient has a history of stroke, hypertension, diabetes, and now with LVH per ECG. Also, a documented concern for possible complications related to infarction based on previous strokes.

Latest vitals: BP 182/129, pulse 73, spo2 94, RR 16. Oxygen administered at room air only. Patient presenting with a possible ongoing headache and unusual mental status, being transported per protocol.
Transport classification assigned as emergent due to neurological concern and stroke protocol.
Patient is altered, with confusion about time and place; complains only of headache and dizziness after recent episode of apparent neurological disruption.
ED staff, this is an active Stroke Alert on an 83-year-old with a prior stroke diagnosis and possible left ventricular hypertrophy.
Patient had a complaint of headache and dizziness starting 30 minutes ago; consistent with stroke alert protocol.
Moving from stretcher to hospital bed using sheet draw method with ED assistance present.
Patient received full PPE notations and stroke alert documentation on scene as per policy.
All gear and patient charts needed are ready for the hospital environment.
Care of the patient has been transferred with ED staff signature to verify handover at the time of arrival.
All meds and paperwork transferred to hospital personnel as required by admissions process.
GCS 13, pupils constricted and unreactive; no neurological deficit signaled during initial assessment.
Red flag for neurological emergency; moved with urgency as required by protocol for active stroke alert.
Final observation remains consistent with acute neurological disorder process.
No signs of new secondary injuries identified in transport or during EMS provision of care.
Diagnosis tentatively marked as stroke and/or related neurological condition, pendinghospital evaluation.
All information has been shared with ED staff via Stroke Alert platform.
Hospital receiving team is fully briefed and ready for immediate assessment as needed.
All handoff forms are complete; patient now under care at receiving facility.
The VCU admits her triage now under the care of ED nursing and physician staffing as requested.
All procedures are completed and transferred, as per approved care delivery for emergent neurological concerns.
EMS diagnosis suspects stroke and passed to ED with staff in place under Stroke Alert communication method.
EMS crew and ED team completed the patient transfer to VCU at this time.
Patient is being treated for headache as possible stroke alert.
EMS has completed its duties with full transfer of care now at the receiving hospital premises.
Wheeling the patient now from ambulance to ED bed at VCU without complications to transport tool or patient.
All tools and methods used in transport have been documented and no incident noted in any part of movement.
Patient transfer to the ED bed is complete with no incident to health or movement status.
All charts and initial transport records have been handed to hospital team as expressly required by protocol.
ED has received her with priority level and is readying the appropriate suite for medical interventional work now.
Collecting all needed information for transfer directly to receiving hospital as part of formal handover process.
Documents are matched with event and properly distributed at the receiving end per standard procedures.
No additional care needed at this moment as reported in all EMS documentation and handover archiving.
EMS crew and ED accepted all documentation needed for continued admissions at this point in time.
No further steps from EMS required, handover finalized and call marked as complete.
The patient is now in the ED and under evaluation for possible neurological issues, including suspected clot.
EMS remains cleared and prepared for next assignment due to call completion.
No unknown conditions reported by EMS to hospital team at point of handover.
Condition at arrival remains consistent with transport, deemed normal as per hospital admissions protocol.
Further neurological follow-up and diagnostics will be conducted by hospital staff upon arrival.
No signs of decline in patient condition during transport or post-handover with hospital.
The patient is under ED monitoring for potential neurological complications.
No adverse outcomes were observed during the time under EMS and transport.
Ambulance crew ensured use of full PPE and protocols for safe transfer.
No known direct signs of transmission risk detected in patient.
All procedures performed according to protocol.
